body:
Dear Arbor Education,

I am applying for the Engineering Director role. I have two children whose school runs on Arbor, so I interact with the platform as a parent regularly. That gives me something most engineering candidates will not have: a direct, personal stake in making the product better, and firsthand knowledge of how it works (and where it frustrates) from the end-user side.

Beyond that personal connection, I have spent 13 years building and leading engineering teams across fintech, blockchain, and developer platforms. Complex systems that serve real people at scale is exactly the kind of infrastructure challenge I find most rewarding.

Your listing describes someone who leads multiple teams through rapid scaling, partners with Product to balance commercial delivery against technical sustainability, and understands how AI-first engineering will reshape the SDLC. That is what I have been doing across my last three roles.

Specific evidence mapped to your requirements:

- Managing and growing managers and senior ICs — At CakeDefi (Series A, $250M AUM), I founded the Moonshot team from scratch, grew it from 1 to 10 engineers, hired and mentored engineering leads, and ran sprint cadence, roadmap ownership, and stakeholder reporting across product and executive layers.

- Leading through organisational shifts and rapid scaling — At Neuroware, I built the technical department from zero: recruited 11 engineers across frontend, backend, and blockchain disciplines, established engineering process, and delivered production systems while the company scaled from seed through institutional partnerships (HSBC, Standard Chartered, EY pilots).

- Balancing commercial value with technical sustainability — My entire CTO tenure at Neuroware (7 years) involved constant negotiation between bank-mandated compliance requirements and engineering velocity. I established architectural patterns that survived three complete pivots of the business model without requiring full rewrites.

- Using data and metrics as a carrot, not a stick — I run sprint planning and delivery tracking as visibility tools for the team, not management oversight. At CakeDefi, engineering metrics surfaced blockers early and gave ICs ownership of their improvement trajectory.

- AI-first engineering with no playbook — I am currently building an AI-augmented development methodology in production: LLM-driven development with automated code review, rule-based quality systems, and MCP tool integration. This is not theoretical; I shipped a full game engine ecosystem (535 commits, 10 repos, 16 AI-callable tools) using this approach in 16 days. I can bring direct, operational experience of leading teams into AI-assisted workflows.

- Technical credibility with senior ICs — I still write production code daily. My recent open-source work (protocol design, SDK architecture, MCP servers) demonstrates current technical depth alongside leadership capability. I am not a manager who stopped engineering.

On the gaps: my teams have been 10-15 engineers rather than 7+ teams of managers. What I bring instead is the rare combination of someone who has built engineering organisations from zero multiple times, shipped at institutional scale (banking, regulated finance), and is currently operating at the frontier of AI-augmented development. I scale teams by establishing strong architectural foundations and clear ownership, not by adding management layers.

I am based in West Yorkshire, available immediately, and the remote arrangement works perfectly. My portfolio is at smalley.my and I have attached my CV.

Kind regards,
Mark Smalley
